text: Simon's attention scarcity thesis inverts the intuition that more choice is always better. Classical economics celebrates choice as welfare-enhancing. But if every choice requires attentional processing, and attention is finite, then multiplying choices multiplies the cognitive tax. Psychologist Barry Schwartz formalized this as the 'paradox of choice' — more options produce less satisfaction and more paralysis. Simon's structural critique runs deeper: it is not merely that excess choice feels overwhelming, but that the infrastructure required to evaluate it consumes the very resource — sustained attention — that is needed for any deliberate, values-based decision.
question: According to the passage, what does Simon's analysis add to Barry Schwartz's paradox of choice?

options.text: A neurological account of why options cause emotional distress
Evidence that choice satisfaction increases once options exceed a threshold
The insight that evaluating choices consumes the attention needed for values-based decisions
A reminder that classical economics already accounted for cognitive limits
